Sources.Title: Mission
Sources.Content:
# Mission — aiarch

A platform that produces predictably-engineered software and, for a usage-based fee, builds and hosts it for the user.

## Vision

Design and build a collection of software components that together: (a) carry a customer through The Method as a guided workflow, (b) orchestrate, dispatch, and supervise the automated work — each LLM-needing activity dispatched as an agentic-coding job to the user's own CI (a GitHub Action running claude-code-action on the user's Claude Code token), aiarch holding no code text and no LLM key and reading results back from the user's git, (c) deploy and operate the delivered system on managed infrastructure (in the operated scenario), and (d) bill the user a usage-based hosting fee for operated systems only — no flat subscription, no charge for construction; the user's own Claude Code account pays for all LLM work. The mission is to orchestrate, not to author code or hold tokens.

## Business Objectives

1. Predictable delivery — customers receive a duration, cost, and risk estimate before any code is written, and the delivered system arrives within the bounds of the chosen option.
2. Method conformance — every project that ships through the platform is the output of a complete pass through business alignment, requirements scrubbing, volatility-based decomposition, project design with options, and reviewed construction — no skipped phases.
3. Usage-based hosting vendor revenue — aiarch earns by billing the user a usage-based hosting fee when it operates the delivered system (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only). Construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account and GitHub Actions — aiarch does not charge for construction or LLM work. The only metered, aiarch-borne cost is hosting.
4. Customer self-sufficiency, platform dependency — the customer walks through The Method themselves but does not need engineering staff: the platform's AI Workers play every technical Method role while the customer remains the architect-of-record and requirements source.
5. Cost transparency at decision time — the projected hosting cost (usage-based, DEPLOYED — OPERATED only) is presented as a traceable figure whenever a customer is asked to commit money; there is no flat subscription.
6. Extensibility to new categories of work — the platform can absorb new kinds of work products and new kinds of automated workers without re-architecting.
7. Audit and intervention — a human can take over, replay, or override any in-flight project; nothing the platform does is opaque or unrecoverable.
8. Drivable by the user's own AI agent and the user's own CI — aiarch is shaped so that the user drives it with their own AI agent (over the remote MCP channel) and so that all the work that needs an LLM or a build runs on the user's own CI (a GitHub Action running the agentic-coding job on the user's CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN and CI minutes).

## Key Design Decisions (as of 2026-06-14)

- G1: Fast onboarding — users get on the platform via GitHub + their existing Claude Code account.
- G2: Zero LLM spend by aiarch — the user's Claude Code token pays for all LLM work inside the agentic Action; aiarch never holds an LLM key and never pays per-token.
- G3: No code-tooling to build — claude-code-action already does read/edit/search/build; aiarch orchestrates, it does not author or hold code.
- Delivery collapses to cloud-only (operate on/off) plus a test profile. The customer-facing LOCAL deployment is retired.
- Every LLM-needing activity (Phase-1 drafting, Phase-2 plan drafting, Phase-3 construction) is dispatched as an agentic-coding job to the user's own CI.
- Project state and all persisted artifacts live in the user's own GitHub repo (via a GitHub App), not in an aiarch-managed database.

## Deployment Scenarios

| Scenario | Deploy | Git target | Durable exec | Agentic-coding CI | Operate/host | Billing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DEPLOYED — NOT OPERATED | aiarch cloud | user's GitHub (App) | Temporal | user's GitHub Actions + claude-code-action | — | — (construction on user's own Claude/GH-Actions token; not billed) |
| DEPLOYED — OPERATED | aiarch cloud | user's GitHub (App) | Temporal | user's GitHub Actions + claude-code-action | ArgoCD/Rollouts → aiarch cluster | usage-based hosting |
| TEST PROFILE (internal) | in-process, ephemeral | ephemeral throwaway repo | embedded executor | local exec runner / stubbed | stubbed | none |

Sources.Title: Founder Brief
Sources.Content:
# Founder Brief — aiarch

Verbatim founder input that seeded this product. Captured 2026-05-13.

## The pitch

"I want to get meta and create an app for creating apps. My real business will be a cloud computing platform like GCP/Azure/AWS, but the draw is a killer app for building quality software predictably with The Method and AI agents with easy deployment. Mixofreality will run the k8s cluster, manage CI (Argo Workflows), manage deployments (Argo CD), and host a UI portal & MCP server for building your app. We will pay to host and charge you $$$ over for it. As mentioned before, the app is essentially just The Method. You use MCP or the UI to walk through all the steps of The Method, chatting with an AI expert on The Method as an architectural sidekick. It then plans projects for you and estimates time & cost based on AI agent costs to implement the project. Really we're just swapping out senior/jr engineers from The Method for AI agents. All the same philosophy applies."

## Key implications

- Two intertwined offerings, one product surface: (a) a guided 'build your app via The Method' experience, (b) a managed cloud where the resulting app runs. The guided experience is the wedge; hosting is the recurring revenue.
- The Method is the differentiator. Competing IDE/agent products (Cursor, Devin, v0, Lovable, Replit Agent) skip directly to code. aiarch enforces Phase 1 → Phase 2 → Phase 3 discipline: business alignment, requirements scrubbing, volatility-based decomposition, project design with options, then construction.
- AI agents replace senior/junior engineers. The Method's hand-off model (architect + senior + junior) maps 1:1 onto agent roles. Tasks are scoped the same way; the human's role shifts to architect/PM.
- Cost estimation is now agent-cost estimation. Project design produces duration + cost estimates; cost is denominated in agent-call $$, not engineer-salary $$.
- Deployment is push-button. Output of construction = deployable artifacts + helm charts. Argo CD picks them up. The user doesn't manage k8s; aiarch does.
- Margin is on hosting, not on the build experience.

## Known customer archetypes

- Solo technical founders who want to build a SaaS without hiring a team.
- Small product/eng leaders inside larger orgs who want a sanctioned path to ship a new internal tool quickly with quality.
- Consultancies that already practice The Method and want leverage over their per-engagement cost.

## Competitive landscape

aiarch's bet: opinionated, Method-driven build experience + managed hosting is a niche neither agent IDEs (Cursor/Devin/Aider — no architecture discipline, no hosting) nor vibe-coding tools (v0/Bolt/Lovable — fast but not maintainable) nor pure managed hosting (Vercel/Render/Fly.io — no build experience) currently serves.

model.vision: A platform that produces predictably-engineered software and, for a usage-based fee, builds and hosts it for the user.

objectives.statement: Predictable delivery: customers receive a duration, cost, and risk estimate before any code is written, and the delivered system arrives within the bounds of the chosen option.

Method conformance: every project that ships through the platform is the output of a complete pass through business alignment, requirements scrubbing, volatility-based decomposition, project design with options, and reviewed construction — no skipped phases.

Usage-based hosting vendor revenue: aiarch earns by billing the user a usage-based hosting fee when it operates the delivered system (DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario only); there is no flat subscription; construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account and GitHub Actions — aiarch does not charge for construction or LLM work; the only metered, aiarch-borne cost is hosting/compute; aiarch is a pure service vendor and is merchant of record only for its own invoices.

Customer self-sufficiency, platform dependency: the customer walks through The Method themselves but does not need engineering staff — the platform's AI Workers play every technical Method role (architect, senior, junior, test, ops) while the customer remains the architect-of-record and requirements source.

Cost transparency at decision time: the projected hosting cost (usage-based, DEPLOYED — OPERATED only) is presented as a traceable figure whenever a customer is asked to commit money, including a usage-to-hosting-cost forecast per option; there is no flat subscription line.

Extensibility to new categories of work: the platform can absorb new kinds of work products and new kinds of automated workers without re-architecting; the Method itself remains stable as the infrastructure beneath them changes.
Audit and intervention: a human can take over, replay, or override any in-flight project; nothing the platform does is opaque or unrecoverable.

Drivable by the user's own AI agent and the user's own CI: aiarch is shaped so that the user drives it with their own AI agent (over the remote MCP channel — the full human Manager surface as tools) and so that all work needing an LLM or a build runs on the user's own CI (a GitHub Action running the agentic-coding job on the user's CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN and CI minutes); delivery collapses to one cloud topology with operate on/off plus a test profile.

model.mission: Design and build a collection of software components that together: (a) carry a customer through The Method as a guided workflow, (b) orchestrate, dispatch, and supervise the automated work — each LLM-needing activity dispatched as an agentic-coding job to the user's own CI (a GitHub Action running claude-code-action on the user's Claude Code token), aiarch holding no code text and no LLM key and reading results back from the user's git, (c) deploy and operate the delivered system on managed infrastructure (in the operated scenario), and (d) bill the user a usage-based hosting fee for operated systems only — no flat subscription, no charge for construction or LLM work (the user's own Claude Code account pays for all LLM work); the mission is to orchestrate, not to author code or hold tokens; specific build experiences and operational offerings are assembled from those components.

items.definition: The platform's counterparty and the party aiarch bills. Owns one or more projects; pays aiarch, usage-based, for hosting of operated systems (DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario only); construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account and GitHub Actions — aiarch does not charge for construction or LLM work. The user owns their code and is merchant of record for their own app — aiarch never collects or shares in the user's end-customer revenue. May be a solo founder, an internal product/eng lead, or a consultancy.

The customer-side person who drives a project through The Method. The Architect User is the architect-of-record (decision-maker and requirements source) but typically not an engineer; they may be a non-technical founder, internal product lead, or hobbyist. The platform's AI Worker plays the role of architectural expert / Method sidekick — drafting, guiding, validating — but the Architect User commits every gate.
A mixofreality staff member with authority to intervene in a running project or operating system.

Any entity that produces work products on behalf of a project. The default is an AI agent. A Worker may also be a human contractor sourced through aiarch's contractor marketplace. Multiple Worker classes may coexist on one project (planner Worker, coder Worker, reviewer Worker), and AI and human Workers may be mixed.

A person who uses a customer's deployed application. Pays the customer (the customer is merchant of record for their own app); aiarch never sees or touches End-User payments. From aiarch's perspective an End User is invisible — they interact only with the customer's deployed system, not with aiarch.

The software the platform built and is now operating on the customer's behalf (DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario only). A runtime subject: it emits telemetry and consumes hosting capacity, which aiarch meters and bills the customer for.
Produce the validated artifacts that constitute system design and project design for a customer's intended system, including options for management decision-making.
Drive the construction phase to completion: dispatch work, supervise progress, replan on variance.
Host, run, scale, and observe the system the project produced (DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario only).

Meter the hosting/compute a customer consumed over a billing period (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only), price it per the current ServicePricing, and charge the customer's stored instrument for aiarch's own service invoice. One-directional (aiarch charges the user; there is no payout). Construction LLM work is never metered by aiarch — the user's own Claude Code account pays for that.
Pause, replay, override, or take over an in-flight project or operated system.
Capture or refine a single Method artifact (mission, glossary, volatilities, etc.) with the architect user in the loop.
Produce duration, build cost, and risk for one project option, given an activity network and a worker mix. Does not include operating-cost forecasts (see Operation Estimation).

Produce the usage-to-operating-cost curve and payout-vs-shortfall forecast for one project option (at SDP-review time, given the customer's declared usage assumptions) or for one in-flight Operated System (at op-time, given observed telemetry plus a set of scale what-if points).

A read-side activity: surface the current run-rate, the projected monthly cost, and a what-if curve for an Operated System so the Operator and Customer can see how cost scales with load. Powered by Operation Estimation over observed usage; no state mutation.
Assign a discrete construction activity to a worker and supervise it to completion or failure.
Bind an operated system to runtime capacity (compute, storage, network) on the platform's managed infrastructure.

The platform's own decision to change an Operated System's desired capacity (scale up, scale down, idle-pause, resume-to-baseline) from observed telemetry, per the customer's AutoscalerPolicy. Same mechanism as Operator-driven scaling (publishes revised desired state); different decider. Fires inside the same reconcile tick that does observation, so there is no separate autoscaler schedule.

The Autoscaling decision to publish replicas=0 for an Operated System after a threshold of inactivity (no inbound traffic for AutoscalerPolicy.idleThreshold). The app is held at zero replicas at no compute cost; routing remains live behind the platform's edge.

An infrastructure-side capability (Envoy + a scale-from-zero controller such as KEDA HTTP scaler or Knative) that intercepts the first inbound request to an idle-paused app, holds the request, raises replicas to baseline, and forwards. The next reconcile tick observes the new actual state. Not a platform-side activity; an Operated Runtime infrastructure property exposed through OperatedRuntimeAccess.

Convert observed operation events into a billable quantity: hosting/compute consumption attributed to the customer for their service invoice (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only). Construction-token consumption is not metered by aiarch — that is the user's own Claude Code account.

The act of pricing a customer's hosting/compute usage for a billing period and charging the resulting amount to the customer's stored instrument (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only). One-directional (aiarch charges the user; there is no payout, no netting against any inbound revenue). No flat subscription; no charge for construction or LLM work.
Given a stalled or off-track project, an unhealthy operated system, or a failed service invoice (declined charge), determine whether to retry, escalate to a human, or take over.

Umbrella term for one typed Method work product belonging to a project (Phase 1/2). Recorded in Project State; carries a review status (awaiting / committed / rejected / withdrawn). Lives in the projectstate Go package; Engines and Managers import it as legal downward edges.

The typed representation of a system designed with the Method: its components (Client / Manager / Engine / ResourceAccess / Resource / Utility) and the relationships among them, over which the book's call-legality and cardinality rules are expressible. The SPA renders it client-side as a React-Flow C4 component graph.

The typed representation of a use case and its activity diagram: actors/roles, trigger, core-vs-non-core classification, and the activity-diagram nodes/edges the book permits (start, action, decision, fork/join, merge, swimlane, end). The SPA renders it client-side as a React-Flow activity/lane graph.

Apply an atomic business-verb mutation to the project's head-state aggregate (e.g. commit/reject/withdraw an artifact, advance the phase). The head-state carries the typed Method artifact models themselves for Phase 1/2 (the canonical definitions). As of 2026-06-09 project state is serialized as JSON files in the project's git repo (e.g. under .aiarch/state/), mutated in place; optimistic concurrency is git ref compare-and-swap (a push rejected on non-fast-forward → reload + retry the verb), and idempotency is an in-repo applied_mutation record.
Read the project's current head-state aggregate from the project's git repo.

Deposit a Phase-3 construction output (source code, build artifact, chart, manifest) into the project's git repo (commit-addressable). As of 2026-06-15 this is projectStateAccess's 2nd contract (Phase-3 construction-output staging), not a separate artifactAccess seam.
Fetch a previously staged construction output by its content address (commit SHA) from the project's git repo (projectStateAccess contract 2; 2026-06-15 — was artifactAccess).

Produce a human-facing rendering of a typed Method artifact model. As of 2026-06-01 this is a client concern: the server returns the typed model and the React SPA renders it (React-Flow C4/activity graph, SVG volatility scatter, react-markdown prose). The rendering is a derivation, never the source of truth.

Create, list, and read a project as a catalog entry (the projects landing grid + the per-project home base). The new thin projectManager (CreateProject / ListProjects / GetProject) owns this; CreateProject is the explicit project birth. A non-core use case encapsulating the ProjectCatalog volatility.

submitConstructionPipeline(spec) on constructionPipelineAccess. As of 2026-06-14 the dispatched job is an agentic-coding job — a GitHub Action running claude-code-action that checks out the branch, has the agent do the task on the user's Claude Code token, runs the repo validation step, and commits / opens a PR.
Read status, step logs, run result, and (for an agentic job) the committed result / opened PR of a submitted job.
Apply the desired state of an operated system to managed runtime capacity.
Release the runtime capacity bound to an operated system.
Read per-customer compute/hosting cost from the operated runtime's telemetry / FinOps surface (for the hosting portion of the user's bill).

Append a billable hosting/compute usage event (operation) to the usage log. As of 2026-06-14 token metering dropped — the user's Claude Code account pays for LLM work, so there are no construction tokens for aiarch to meter.
Read a customer's metered hosting usage over a billing period.

Submit an authorized payment request against the customer's stored instrument for an aiarch service invoice (hosting/compute usage, DEPLOYED — OPERATED only). One-directional vendor charge; aiarch is merchant of record for its own invoices only. No flat subscription; construction is not billed by aiarch.

Install/authorize the GitHub App on the user's GitHub org and provision the per-project repo. As of 2026-06-14 also: writeActionsSecret (store the user's CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN as a repo Actions secret in the user's own repo — aiarch holds no LLM key) and commitAgenticWorkflowFile (commit the claude-code-action workflow .yml into the user's repo). The thin sourceControlAccess ResourceAccess owns these.

items.term: Open / Update / Close a Work Item
items.definition: openWorkItem(activity) → WorkItemRef / updateWorkItem(ref, {status, assignee, comment}) / closeWorkItem(ref) on the new workItemAccess ResourceAccess (encapsulates the WorkTracking volatility). Mirrors a Method activity to the customer's work-item tracker (GitHub Issue today → Jira/Linear later). Write-mostly v1 — the Method network is the source of truth; the work item is a one-directional mirror.

recordActivityWorkItemOpened(activityId, WorkItemRef) on projectStateAccess: records the opened work item's ref onto the activity head-state, paralleling the git/PR head-state verbs. This is where the activity's two composing external head-states (issue + PR) are joined — at the Manager/projectStateAccess level, NOT by an RA→RA call.

The single per-project Git repo that holds everything aiarch persists: project state (the typed Phase-1/2 Method models serialized as JSON, e.g. under .aiarch/state/) and Phase-3 construction outputs (source, charts, manifests) in the same repo tree. As of 2026-06-15 fronted by projectStateAccess alone — one ResourceAccess with two contracts over the one Resource.

The head-state record of each project's current lifecycle state (phase + per-artifact review status) and the typed Method artifact models for Phase 1/2 (the canonical definitions), serialized as JSON in the Project Git Repo and mutated in place under git ref CAS.

The agentic-coding-job + pipeline-execution infrastructure used during drafting + construction. This is the user's own GitHub Actions running claude-code-action (per-job containers with resource classes, actions/cache + artifact passing; the agent does the task on the user's Claude Code token and commits / opens a PR); in the test profile, a local exec runner / stub.

The infrastructure that runs operated systems (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only): Kubernetes via Argo CD + Argo Rollouts, watching the manifests in the user's own GitHub repo and reconciling the aiarch hosting cluster. Receives deployments, reports health, surfaces per-customer compute/hosting attribution.

The durable record of metered hosting/compute usage events (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only), used to compute the customer's service bill. Construction-token consumption is not recorded here — the user's own Claude Code account handles that.

The user's own external system. aiarch installs a GitHub App on the user's org to provision the per-project repo and run construction via GitHub Actions in the user's account. aiarch orchestrates via the GitHub API; the user owns the repos and CI minutes.

The external system that charges the user for aiarch's own service invoices (hosting/compute usage, DEPLOYED — OPERATED only). Today: Stripe used as an ordinary vendor-to-customer charge (NOT Connect-as-MoR-for-end-users). aiarch is merchant of record only for its own invoices. No token metering; no flat subscription.

The external project-management SaaS where Method activities are mirrored as work items. Instance: GitHub Issues today → Jira / Linear / Azure Boards. Fronted by workItemAccess (the WorkTracking volatility). Independent seam from GitHub (repos/CI) — a customer may run GitHub repos + Jira issues, or GitLab + Linear.

A bounded time window over which a customer's metered hosting/compute consumption (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only) is accumulated for a single service invoice. Cadence is an aiarch operational parameter. No flat subscription line; no construction-token line — the user's own Claude Code account pays for all LLM work, and aiarch charges only for hosting.

How aiarch prices its own service to the user. 2026-06-14 instance: a hosting rate for operated systems (tier floors, free-tier subsidies — all Strategy parameters; no token unit price, no flat subscription). The ServicePricing volatility (axis 1); billingEngine pivots on it as a Strategy. Applies only in the DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario.

The amount a customer owes aiarch for a billing period (hosting/compute usage, DEPLOYED — OPERATED only), charged to the customer's stored instrument via the Billing Gateway (Stripe as an ordinary vendor charge). No flat subscription; construction is not billed by aiarch. aiarch is merchant of record only for its own invoices.

A declined service-invoice charge. Retried per the billing schedule; on persistent decline it becomes an InterventionPolicy trigger (operator escalation; possible pause/withdraw of the operated system per intervention policy).

The customer's declared expectation of end-user load on a deployed app (e.g., expected daily active users, requests per minute, average payload size, peak-to-trough ratio). Captured during project design as part of planning assumptions; used by Operation Estimation at SDP-review time to produce a usage-to-operating-cost curve so the customer can forecast their hosting bill.

A function plotting projected hosting cost against load level (e.g., monthly compute cost at 1×, 2×, 5×, 10× the declared or observed usage). Shown at SDP-review time per project option so the customer sees how their hosting bill evolves with success; also shown at op-time per Operated System against observed usage for live cost visibility.

The unit of LLM-needing work in aiarch as of 2026-06-14: a GitHub Action run that executes claude-code-action against the user's project repo. Dispatched by a Manager via constructionPipelineAccess.submitConstructionPipeline (workflow_dispatch with the activity's prompt / branch / contract); the agent does the task on the user's Claude Code token, runs the repo validation step, and commits / opens a PR. Replaces the synchronous workerAccess call across Phase-1/2 drafting AND Phase-3 construction. Async — minutes per run.

The GitHub Action that runs the coding agent inside the user's CI. The current instance of the agentic-coding-job runtime (WorkflowRuntime face-1). Future instances: GitHub Copilot coding agent, GitLab CI — same dispatch/observe shape, different workflow body + credential mechanics.

The user's own Claude Code account credential, stored as a repo Actions secret in the user's own repo (written by sourceControlAccess.writeActionsSecret). It pays for all LLM work done by the agentic job (Goal G2 — zero LLM spend by aiarch; the user does not double-pay). The token never touches aiarch infra; the Action runs on the user's CI minutes.

The mirror of a Method activity in the customer's tracker (a GitHub Issue today). Opened when the Phase-2 activity network seals (openWorkItem), updated during construction (updateWorkItem), closed on merge (closeWorkItem). Write-mostly v1 — the Method network (git-JSON) is the source of truth; the work item is a one-directional projection. An activity carries two composing external head-states: its work item (issue) and its PR (deliverable).

The opaque handle to a work item returned by openWorkItem. Recorded onto the activity head-state via projectStateAccess.recordActivityWorkItemOpened; projected to the SPA alongside the gitRows. Activity-id → ref is name-as-identity (server-resolved; no LLM-minted ids).

A named bundle of instance selections across the durable-execution, client/transport, git-target, agentic-coding-CI, work-item-tracker, and service-pricing seams, plus an operation on/off flag, chosen at process start. Configuration, not a volatility. Two production scenarios + a test profile as of 2026-06-14: DEPLOYED — NOT OPERATED, DEPLOYED — OPERATED, and the internal Test Profile.

Hosted in aiarch's cloud through construction (and change-requests), but aiarch does not host the result: Temporal for durable execution, web UI behind cluster ingress + a remote MCP endpoint, the user's own GitHub (via a GitHub App) as the git target, GitHub Actions running claude-code-action for the agentic-coding CI, the user's GitHub Issues for work tracking. No aiarch billing in this scenario — construction runs on the user's own Claude Code token and CI minutes, and there is no operated system to charge for. The operations Manager / operated runtime / hosting billing are simply not instantiated.

As DEPLOYED — NOT OPERATED, plus aiarch hosts the result: ArgoCD/Rollouts sync the user's GitHub manifests to aiarch's hosting cluster. Billed usage-based for hosting/compute only — no flat subscription, no construction charges. The full revenue path.

The in-process, ephemeral mirror used for fast, no-cluster tests: the Embedded Durable Executor, an ephemeral throwaway git repo, a local exec-runner / stubbed agentic job, and stubbed external SaaS (github, billingGateway, workItemTracker, workerProvider via the test-double workerAccess). Not a customer scenario.

The single-process instance of the durable-execution runtime behind durableExecutionAccess, used in the test profile (vs Temporal in the production scenarios). Satisfies the same frozen verb set (execute / signal / query / timer / schedule / spawn) and the same replay/determinism semantics.

The mechanism by which aiarch operates on the user's own GitHub org: installed/authorized on the user's account, it provisions the per-project repo, writes the CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN Actions secret + commits the claude-code-action workflow file (2026-06-14), and dispatches/observes the agentic-coding runs (GitHub Actions). aiarch never owns the repos or CI minutes; the user does. Fronted by the thin sourceControlAccess ResourceAccess.

The git provider that holds everything aiarch persists (project state JSON + code + manifests), selected per scenario: the user's own GitHub (cloud) vs. the user's local on-disk repo (LOCAL). The GitTarget volatility (renamed from ArtifactTarget 2026-06-09; Gitea removed). Encapsulated by projectStateAccess + artifactAccess + operatedRuntimeAccess swapping which target they front.

The projection of every human-accessible Manager method as an MCP tool, so an AI agent drives the platform as a human would. Generated from the same per-Manager OpenAPI specs as the REST clients (a Client-generation rule, not a volatility). Used by both MCP instances (stdio in LOCAL, HTTP in the cloud scenarios).

The rule that durableExecutionAccess's verb set is frozen and exposes no runtime-specific primitive (GetVersion, reset, search attributes), so the same Manager code runs unchanged on Temporal (cloud) or the Embedded Durable Executor (LOCAL). What keeps deployment scenarios configuration rather than a volatility.

milestones.name: Dog-food transition: use aiarch to drive aiarch's own next iteration

model.rationale: Hold New-decompressed-normal (option a) with re-anchored tracking. PASS-3 recompute confirms the CP is invariant, total duration is invariant, all admitted scope since acceptance is off-CP, and the near-critical set is unchanged. Options (b)/(c) buy 15–40 sim-days that the calendar projection says are not needed, at +0.07/+0.13 composite risk on a CP front (C-CW, I-RA, integration ladder) where risk protection matters most. Option (d) is the standard Directive-7 contrast — longer, costlier, and riskier than the decompressed option on every dimension. The decompressed envelope is conservative: remaining work uses ≈42% of the remaining envelope even at the slow end of the projection range.

items.statement: The platform must carry a customer through the production of a system without that customer assembling tools themselves.
The architect user must have a conversational surface for each Method artifact in which they can co-author with an automated assistant.
The platform must enforce phase ordering and gate progression on artifact validity.
The platform must produce, for a planned system, multiple options (normal, decompressed, compressed, subcritical) with duration, cost, and risk.
Cost estimation must be sensitive to which workers will perform which activities.
The construction hand-off model is policy: which kind of worker handles which Method role (architect / senior / junior).
The platform must accept architect-user interaction over more than one entry channel.
The platform must deploy and operate delivered systems on managed runtime, executing build and deploy through orchestrated workflows.
The platform must produce, for a completed project, an artifact bundle deployable to managed runtime without manual handoff.

aiarch bills the user for hosting of operated systems on a usage-based basis (no flat subscription); construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account and GitHub Actions — aiarch does not charge for construction or LLM work. aiarch is merchant of record only for its own invoices and never touches end-user revenue.
Customer types are heterogeneous and will grow; the platform must not bake assumptions about customer size, internal team, or sophistication into the architecture.
The estimation engine must accept worker-model as an input parameter and emit an estimate per choice.
aiarch invoices the user, usage-based, for hosting of operated systems — no flat subscription, no charge for construction or LLM work; it does not invoice for, or share in, end-user revenue.

Everything aiarch persists — project state (typed Method models as JSON) and source/charts/manifests — lives in one git target per scenario: the user's own GitHub (via a GitHub App) in the cloud scenarios; aiarch orchestrates via the GitHub API and the user owns the repo and their code outright.

The platform orchestrates construction; the build/test/push runs on the user's own GitHub Actions (cloud) or a local runner (LOCAL), dispatched and observed by aiarch via the ConstructionPipelineAccess seam.
The platform must support pause, replay, override, and human takeover of any in-flight construction activity.
MCP is an entry-channel whose internal decomposition is a Client-layer choice, not an architectural pillar; read/write split is an internal MCP-server concern, not a system-level component.
The platform is implemented inside the existing monorepo; the tech stack of internal components is constrained but is not a requirement of the system being built.

The platform must automatically adjust an Operated System's capacity (scale up, scale down, idle-pause, resume-to-baseline) from observed telemetry, per a customer-tunable policy; the autoscaler publishes revised desired state and never drives convergence directly.

The platform must expose, for any Operated System, the current cost run-rate, projected monthly cost, and a what-if scale curve, so the Operator and the Customer can see how operating cost responds to load.
Project estimation at SDP-review time must include a usage-to-operating-cost curve and a sensitivity band for ± uncertainty around the customer's declared usage assumptions, per project option.

Customer apps deployed by aiarch use a curated, evolving set of infrastructure constituents (launch set: Go + Temporal + Postgres + Git + S3); the supported set evolves and worker generation, autoscaling, and cost projection adapt via Strategy registrations on the affected components.

items.name: SystemDesignPhaseWorkflow
items.rationale: The sequence of Phase 1 activities (business alignment → requirements analysis → volatility identification → core use cases → architecture → operational concepts → standard check) will evolve as architectural taste matures: new sub-skills added (security review, accessibility review), existing ones reordered, gating rules changed, artifact schemas refined. A customer completing a project today and starting another in 18 months must encounter a possibly different Phase 1 step set without the platform being rebuilt. Evolves independently of Phase 2.

items.name: ProjectDesignPhaseWorkflow
items.rationale: The sequence of Phase 2 activities (planning assumptions → activity list → network → normal/decompressed/subcritical/compressed → risk modeling → SDP review) will evolve as project-planning sophistication grows: cone-of-uncertainty intervals replacing 5-day quanta, new option types, a new operational-risk curve. Evolves independently of Phase 1 — improvements to the SDP review do not in themselves change requirements scrubbing. Lives on axis 1 because the platform evolves it for everyone at once.

items.name: ConstructionPhaseWorkflow
items.rationale: The sequence of Phase 3 activities (hand-off model → per-component contract design → construction → weekly tracking → scope-change handling) will evolve as construction practice matures: continuous earned-value replacing weekly tracking, automatic contract-drafting at hand-off, AI-driven scope-change proposals. Evolves independently of Phase 1 and Phase 2.

items.name: ProjectCatalog
items.rationale: The platform's project registry — how a project comes into being, who owns it, how projects are listed/discovered for an owner, and the lifecycle states a project moves through before and across the Method phases — will evolve over a customer's tenure: explicit creation today, later templated/forked creation, archival/soft-delete, shared/team ownership, project-level tagging and search, cross-phase status rollups. Distinct from the three phase-workflow volatilities: those encapsulate the step sequence within a phase; ProjectCatalog encapsulates the registry and ownership of the project entity itself. Lives on axis 1 because the platform matures the catalog system-wide for everyone at once.

items.name: ConstructionEstimationModel
items.rationale: How construction duration, cost, and risk are computed will change as workers change, as historical data accumulates, and as the team refines the model. Construction estimation only — the operating-side forecast is split out as OperationEstimationModel. The two evolve on independent timelines (construction estimation matures with worker/build telemetry; operation estimation matures with fleet/usage telemetry).

items.name: OperationEstimationModel
items.rationale: How operating cost is forecasted as a function of usage. Used at design-time (SDP review: usage-to-operating-cost curve + payout-vs-shortfall forecast) and at op-time (OperationsManager: current run-rate, projected monthly cost, what-if curve over observed usage). Evolves as telemetry deepens and as CustomerAppInfrastructure expands. Independent of ConstructionEstimationModel because the inputs differ (usage curves + infrastructure cost model vs. build graph + worker throughput) and the outputs serve different decisions.

items.name: CustomerAppInfrastructure
items.rationale: The list of technologies that a customer's deployed app is permitted to use. Launch set: Go + Temporal + Postgres + Git + S3. The list evolves as the platform broadens (e.g., adding pgvector for AI-feature apps, WASM compute units). The fact of constraint is design given; the contents of the constrained set is what is volatile. Encapsulated by Strategy registrations on AutoscalerEngine, OperationEstimationEngine, and WorkerAccess. Every customer operates on the same permitted set at any given moment — the set broadens for everyone at once.

items.name: ServicePricing
items.rationale: How aiarch prices its own service to the user evolves system-wide over time. Current instance (2026-06-14): a usage-based hosting fee for operated systems only (DEPLOYED — OPERATED); no flat subscription; construction is not charged (runs on the user's own Claude Code token and CI minutes). Tomorrow: per-seat tiers, free-tier subsidies, hosting-resource-class pricing, volume discounts. This is open-ended, will ripple, and must not be a hardcoded constant — it is the policy billingEngine pivots on as a Strategy. Lives on axis 1 because aiarch re-prices its service for everyone as its cost structure and market position change.

items.name: InterventionPolicy
items.rationale: What counts as 'stuck', who is notified, when a human is paged, and what authority they have to override is policy that will change as the platform matures. Trigger surface includes construction variance, unhealthy operated state, and billing failures (a user's stored instrument declines an aiarch service invoice). Early on, every variance escalates to a single operator. Later, severity tiers, customer SLA classes, and self-healing rules emerge.

items.name: AutoscalerPolicy
items.rationale: When and how the platform drives capacity changes (scale up / scale down / idle-pause / resume-to-baseline) on an operated app from observed telemetry. Rules mature on their own timeline: idle-pause thresholds, predictive vs. reactive scaling, SLA-class tiers, scale-from-zero cold-start tolerance, operator overrides. Symmetrical with InterventionPolicy in shape but structurally distinct: InterventionPolicy answers 'is this app broken and what do we do?'; AutoscalerPolicy answers 'is this app's capacity right and what do we do?' Two timelines, two encapsulators.

items.name: ReviewPolicy
items.rationale: Who must review a produced change, from what perspective, and against which reference artifact is policy that will evolve as the platform's engineering practice matures. Today: a code change is reviewed by the owners of each inbound/outbound neighbour component, plus the change's own component owner. Tomorrow: new reviewer roles, adjacency rules keyed on layer or risk, deeper review for higher-risk components, bidirectional amend rules. Lives on axis 1 because the platform evolves the policy system-wide as practice matures. Phase-3 construction only — Phase-1 role choreography is the Manager's fixed sequence.

items.name: ArtifactRendering
items.rationale: The Method's artifacts are typed domain models codified in aiarch/server. What is open-ended is how those canonical models are rendered for human consumption. As of 2026-06-01 this volatility is encapsulated in the client (React SPA), not on the server. The server returns typed models as JSON; the SPA renders them — React-Flow C4 diagrams, a two-axis SVG scatter from the Volatilities model, React-Flow activity diagrams, and markdown prose. The canonical typed model never changes when the renderer does. Lives on axis 1 because the SPA upgrades its rendering system-wide as front-end tooling matures.

items.name: CustomerInteractionChannel
items.rationale: Different customers reach the platform through different entry channels at the same moment. Today: a browser hitting a JSON HTTP API, and an MCP client (remote MCP over HTTP). Tomorrow: IDE plugins, Slack apps, agentic clients, voice. The kind of caller is what varies here. As of 2026-06-14 the customer-facing MCP instance is remote MCP over HTTP only (the MCP-over-stdio instance dropped from the customer surface with the LOCAL story).

items.name: Transport
items.rationale: Even for a fixed channel, the wire-level transport will evolve: JSON-over-HTTP/1.1 today, server-sent-events or WebSockets for streaming draft co-authoring tomorrow; MCP-over-HTTP today, MCP-over-streamable-HTTP tomorrow. Independent of channel: a channel can keep its identity while its transport changes. Each Client component owns sole ownership of its transport stack. Managers never see transport types.

items.name: WorkflowRuntime
items.rationale: Two faces of one volatility: (1) Agentic-coding-job runtime — long-running jobs dispatched via constructionPipelineAccess; current instance is the user's own GitHub Actions running claude-code-action; instance set: claude-code-action → GitHub Copilot coding agent → GitLab CI. (2) Manager-sequencing infrastructure — the durable executor of Manager workflow code; current instance is Temporal; the same Manager code also runs against an in-process embedded executor in the test profile. Both faces encapsulated behind ResourceAccess (constructionPipelineAccess for f1, durableExecutionAccess for f2). Two-axis: at one moment different deployments run different instances (axis 2), and a single deployment migrates its runtime over time (axis 1).

items.name: OperatedRuntime
items.rationale: Same shape as WorkflowRuntime but for the runtime that hosts delivered systems in the DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario. Today: GitOps over Kubernetes via Argo CD + Argo Rollouts on the aiarch hosting cluster. Three faces: convergence infrastructure (how desired-state-in-the-repo becomes actual-state-in-the-cluster), compute cost attribution (per-customer cost-of-goods surface), and runtime-status transport (how the platform learns about state changes in the operated runtime). All three encapsulated behind OperatedRuntimeAccess.

items.name: HandOffPolicy
items.rationale: At any given moment, different customers have chosen different splits of human vs. automated work along the Method's architect / senior / junior hand-off. Some customers want every line of code reviewed by a senior human; others want fully automated construction with the customer acting only as the architect.

items.name: GitTarget
items.rationale: At the same moment, different scenarios persist everything aiarch stores — project state (typed Method models as JSON), Phase-3 construction outputs, and operated-app manifests — to different git targets. Current instances: the user's own GitHub (via a GitHub App, cloud) and an ephemeral throwaway git repo (test). The verbs are stable (createProject / readProject / commit / stageArtifact / retrieveArtifact); the target behind the one per-project Git repo Resource is what varies. Encapsulated by projectStateAccess and operatedRuntimeAccess swapping which git target they front.

items.name: WorkTracking
items.rationale: At the same moment, different customers track the Method's activities in different work-item systems: GitHub Issues today → Jira / Linear / Azure Boards later. Each Method activity is mirrored as a work item: open-on-seal, status-mirror, close-on-merge. The verbs are stable (openWorkItem / updateWorkItem / closeWorkItem; deferred observeWorkItem); the tracker behind them varies per customer. Independent seam from the repo/CI seams — a customer can run GitHub repo + GitHub Actions + Jira issues, or GitLab + GitLab CI + Linear, with no Manager/Engine-layer change.

decisions.decision: External callers reach in-process Clients through Envoy Gateway; Clients call exactly one Manager per request via Temporal (durableExecutionAccess.startOrSignalWorkflow). No internal message bus; no synchronous HTTP between Managers. A durable-execution runtime is mandatory for every Manager without exception.

Closed layering per App C §3.4. No Client-to-Engine, Client-to-ResourceAccess, or skip-layer edges. One permitted exception: the queued BillingManager-to-OperationsManager applyDelinquencyPolicy Temporal Signal (queued M→M per §3.4c.ii).

Every LLM-needing activity (Phase-1 drafting, Phase-2 plan drafting, Phase-3 construction) is dispatched as an agentic-coding job — a GitHub Action running claude-code-action in the user's own GitHub repo on the user's own CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN and CI minutes. aiarch holds no LLM key, makes no synchronous LLM call, and holds no code text; it orchestrates via constructionPipelineAccess and reads results back from git via projectStateAccess.

decisions.topic: artifact presentation is a client concern
decisions.decision: The server does not render Method artifacts. It exposes typed Method models as JSON; the React SPA renders them client-side (React-Flow C4 graph, two-axis SVG scatter, React-Flow activity diagrams, react-markdown prose). renderingEngine and renderingToolchain are removed. Every typed model becomes part of the frozen wire contract via the OpenAPI discriminated modelEnvelope.

decisions.topic: catalog read — getProject single typed read
decisions.decision: projectManager.getProject(projectId) returns the full typed project head-state in one synchronous read (phase, version, research, every artifact slot with kind/stage/model/findings/critique). Thin pass-through over projectStateAccess.ReadProject; no per-artifact fan-out, no durable workflow. Powers both the home-base TOC and the design-experience render path.

decisions.topic: project state storage — git JSON with ref CAS
decisions.decision: Project state is JSON files in the per-project git repo (behind projectStateAccess), mutated by guarded commits under git ref compare-and-swap (push rejected non-fast-forward → reload + retry). Activity-retry idempotency is an in-repo applied_mutation record keyed on workflowId:activityId. The separate project-state Postgres cluster is removed. Operated-system and billing head-state remain Postgres aggregates with version-guarded UPDATE + applied_mutation dedup.

Every aiarch service is written in Go (latest stable). The unsafe package is forbidden in aiarch source via depguard; transitive unsafe use is audited via SBOM + govulncheck. go test -race runs on every CI test run. Third-party dependencies only when ubiquitous AND absolutely necessary (launch set: go.temporal.io/sdk, github.com/jackc/pgx/v5).

decisions.topic: generated REST clients — Go + TypeScript from one OpenAPI spec
decisions.decision: Every Manager owns one OpenAPI spec. Codegen produces a Go package and a TypeScript npm package per Manager from that spec. The MCP tool surface is a further mechanical projection of the same per-Manager OpenAPI specs (each operation becomes one MCP tool). Both codegen outputs are committed and drift is detected in CI.

decisions.topic: billing model — usage-based cloud hosting only
decisions.decision: aiarch bills the user a usage-based hosting fee for operated systems (DEPLOYED — OPERATED only); there is no flat subscription and construction is not charged (construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account and GitHub Actions). billingEngine pivots on ServicePricing as a Strategy (tier floors, free-tier subsidies; no token unit price). aiarch is merchant of record only for its own service invoices; it never touches end-user revenue.

decisions.topic: deployment scenarios are configuration, not architecture
decisions.decision: Two production scenarios (DEPLOYED — NOT OPERATED; DEPLOYED — OPERATED) differ only by an operation on/off flag — OperationsManager and OperatedRuntime and hosting billing are not instantiated in the not-operated scenario. A test profile swaps the durable-execution runtime to the embedded executor, the git target to an ephemeral throwaway repo, and the agentic-coding CI to a local exec runner or stub. The component graph, call chains, and component count are identical across all scenarios.

items.guideline: Never design against requirements; design against volatility.
Avoid functional decomposition — no container or component named after a feature.
Decompose based on volatility — every component cites a volatilities.md entry.
Composable design — non-core use cases trace through existing components without modification.
Features as integration, not implementation — no Manager named for a feature.
Design iteratively, build incrementally.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; not applicable to system design artifacts.

items.guideline: Design the project to build the system.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; covered in Phase 2 project design.

items.guideline: Educated decisions with options.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; addressed in Phase 2 SDP review.

items.guideline: Build along the critical path.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; addressed in Phase 3 construction.

items.guideline: Be on time throughout.
items.justification: N/A at Phase 1; addressed in Phase 3 tracking.

items.guideline: Capture required behavior, not functionality — requirements state what, not how.
Describe required behavior with use cases — actor, trigger, outcome, success path, alternatives.
Document use cases with nested conditions via activity diagrams.
Eliminate solutions masquerading as requirements — scrubbed-requirements.md complete.
Validate by supporting all core use cases — every core UC has a dynamic view in architecture.dsl.
Rarely more than 6 core use cases.
items.justification: 5 core use cases as of 2026-06-09 (UC5 Onboard Payment Integration removed with merchant-of-record reversal). Cardinality flag resolved; previously at ceiling of 6, now comfortably within bound.

items.guideline: 5 or fewer Managers without subsystems.
items.justification: 6 Managers — the thin projectManager (encapsulates ProjectCatalog volatility: 3 ops, no durable workflow, 0 Engines, 0 Resources) is the 6th. Introducing subsystems solely to avoid +1 would add complexity without payoff. Waiver granted 2026-06-01; no Manager added since. Re-evaluate if count grows past 6.

items.guideline: Few subsystems.
3 or fewer Managers per subsystem.
items.justification: N/A — zero subsystems in use.

items.guideline: Golden Engines-to-Managers ratio — fewer Engines than Managers (the book's expectation).
items.justification: 7 Engines / 6 Managers as of 2026-06-15 (artifactValidationEngine removed — validation re-homes to a required CI check inside the agentic Action). Ratio smell narrowed further toward the book's expectation. Each remaining Engine encapsulates exactly one named activity volatility. Warning, not error; each Engine has a 1:1 volatility-to-encapsulator relationship.

items.guideline: ResourceAccess may serve multiple Resources.
ResourceAccess count within order-of-magnitude bound (~10).
items.justification: 10 production ResourceAccess components as of 2026-06-15: projectStateAccess, operatedSystemStateAccess, billingStateAccess, constructionPipelineAccess, operatedRuntimeAccess, usageAccess, billingGatewayAccess, sourceControlAccess, durableExecutionAccess, workItemAccess. artifactAccess folded into projectStateAccess C2; workerAccess removed entirely. Inside the order-of-magnitude bound.

items.guideline: Resource count within order-of-magnitude bound (~10).
items.justification: 7 internal Resources + 3 external softwareSystems (billingGateway, github, workItemTracker) as of 2026-06-15. workerProvider removed as external system. Inside bound.

items.guideline: Volatility count within 6–15 target range.
items.justification: 18 volatilities as of 2026-06-15 — over the 6–15 upper bound. Each entry has exactly one encapsulator and a business trace; no speculative subdivision. Count flat since 2026-06-14 (WorkTracking +1, Worker subsumed into WorkflowRuntime f1 −1). Accepted: compression candidate (ConstructionEstimationModel + OperationEstimationModel) would violate one-volatility-per-encapsulator if merged, as their evolution timelines are independent.

items.guideline: Volatility decreases top-down through the layers.
Reuse increases top-down — Utilities most reusable, Managers single-purpose.
Do not encapsulate nature-of-business changes.
Managers should be almost expendable — all Engines, RA, Resources, and Utilities survive a Manager rewrite.
Symmetric design — all Managers receive direct Client calls, use Strategy-style Engines, read/write through ResourceAccess, log via Utilities.
No public channels for internal interactions — no internal pub/sub.
Resources serve one purpose — each Resource is fronted by ResourceAccess encapsulating a distinct volatility.
items.justification: projectGitRepo fronted by projectStateAccess alone (one RA, two contracts) as of 2026-06-15. Cleaner than the prior two-RA arrangement: one access component hides the resource; the two write-disciplines are contract boundaries, not separate components.

items.guideline: Avoid open architecture — closed layer model declared.
Avoid semi-closed/semi-open architecture.
Prefer closed layering.
Do not call up — every edge goes within a layer or down to a lower layer.
No sideways edges except queued M↔M or M→E.
No skipping layers — Clients reach Managers directly; Clients do not reach Engine/RA/Resource.
Resolve open architecture attempts via queued or async calls.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied — no sideways open attempts exist.

items.guideline: Extend the system by implementing subsystems.
items.justification: N/A — forward-looking; no subsystems needed at current Manager count.

items.guideline: All components can call Utilities — every Client and Manager calls Security/Logging/Diagnostics.
Managers and Engines can call ResourceAccess; Engines must not do I/O.
items.justification: As of 2026-06-15 no Engine does I/O: artifactValidationEngine (which previously shelled out to a CI check) is removed; renderingEngine (which shelled out to a CLI tool) is removed per 2026-06-01 pivot. All remaining Engines are pure-computation functions of their inputs.

items.guideline: Managers can call Engines — every Manager calls at least one Engine.
Managers can queue calls to another Manager.
items.justification: Permitted but not used — every cross-Manager transition is Client-initiated. No internal bus; no rent paid for a bus. Documented in operational-concepts §1 and §2.

items.guideline: Clients do not call multiple Managers per use case.
Managers do not queue to more than one Manager per use case.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied — Managers do not queue calls at all.

items.guideline: Engines do not receive queued calls.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied — Engines receive only sync in-process calls from Managers.

items.guideline: ResourceAccess does not receive queued calls.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied — RA receives only sync in-process calls from Managers.

items.guideline: Clients do not publish events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied — bus removed. Each Client makes a direct in-process call to exactly one Manager method per inbound request. The prior Don't 6e waiver (Clients publishing onto the bus) is revoked.

items.guideline: Engines do not publish events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied.

items.guideline: ResourceAccess does not publish events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied.

items.guideline: Resources do not publish events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied.

items.guideline: Engines, ResourceAccess, and Resources do not subscribe to events.
items.justification: Trivially satisfied.

items.guideline: Service contracts: per-component op-count within sweet spot (3–5 typical, ≤12 hard cap); ≤2 contracts per ResourceAccess.
items.justification: projectStateAccess carries 2 contracts (≤2 verified per App B §5.4): C1 = typed Phase-1/2 head-state verbs; C2 = Phase-3 construction-output staging/retrieval. sourceControlAccess carries exactly 2 contracts (repo-lifecycle + IPullRequestRail). All RA op-counts within App B bands per contract.

items.guideline: Identical component set in test and production — no test-only production seams.
items.justification: Strengthened 2026-06-15: workerAccess removed entirely (not just demoted to test-only). The LLM is stubbed at the agentic-job boundary (constructionPipelineAccess/sourceControlAccess), not at a workerAccess seam. Internal component set is genuinely identical in test and prod.

items.guideline: Mission integrity and traceability — every volatility has an encapsulator; every objective has a component.
Structurizr DSL validity — architecture.dsl and workspace.dsl validate clean.

Billing model: usage-based cloud hosting charge only; no flat subscription; construction runs on the user's own Claude Code account; aiarch is a pure service vendor, merchant of record for its own invoices only.
items.justification: CON-3 reversed 2026-06-09: aiarch is a pure service vendor. BillingManager + BillingEngine + BillingGatewayAccess + UsageAccess implement one-directional hosting charge (meter hosting/compute → price via ServicePricing → charge the user). No flat subscription; no token metering; no construction charges; no revenue-share.

model.notes: Team: solo founder (bus-factor-1) + up to 3 concurrent Claude agent streams (architect/PM/product-manager/specialist roles). Founder works ~2 effective days/week (40% of nominal). Parallelism cap: 3 in-flight agent streams; beyond that, founder review quality degrades. Infrastructure: Go + Temporal + Postgres (CNPG for Temporal + billing head-state only; project state is JSON-in-git with git-ref CAS). Construction CI is the user's own GitHub Actions running claude-code-action — aiarch holds no LLM key and does not charge for construction. Billing model (2026-06-09 MoR-reversal): pure one-directional vendor charge — aiarch bills the user a usage-based HOSTING fee only (DEPLOYED — OPERATED scenario); no flat subscription; no revenue share; no charge for construction or LLM work; revenueShare=0 (no payout; the old merchant-of-record / revenue-share model was reversed). computeCost=2 (tieredFloors: tier floors + free-tier subsidies per ServicePricing strategy); schedule=1 (monthly billing period). Risk flags: bus-factor-1; founder availability variable (2d/wk); operated runtime unchosen; durable execution platform unchosen; git-as-project-state-store novelty; AI-agent construction quality variance; dog-food chicken-and-egg. No hard external deadlines. Internal-only v1 audience.
